After that process, I would verify his benefits, making sure he has active insurance, making sure that there's no co-pays and things like that. Once that is completed, then we'll schedule the patient for admission. Gotcha, gotcha. Well, he reached out last night and complained about something petty that happened a little while ago. Before he goes through the rest of that, I'm thinking, well, he's got to pay some money to get some help, if he wants it. He's been through a couple other programs, and once you start feeling better, you decide you're going to leave because you know better, and he falls back in the same trap. I don't think he was ready to quit, but he just shattered his femur about a couple months ago. I think a guy pushed him over and stomped him and was stealing from him while he was in the hospital. One of his buddies was taking a couple hundred bucks out of his account every day, because that's all he could get. That's awful. He crashed his car moving to Roanoke, Virginia, like six years ago, and about tore his foot off. His right leg's rods and pins are just above the knee down. You'd think he'd hit bottom at some point, but I'm just trying, if he's willing to go, if there's an option somewhere, but if he's not, he's not. I totally understand the way this works. I would just encourage you, thank you for calling, first of all. He doesn't know that he, or probably don't understand that he has a lot of support, even for you calling for him. Thank you for that. Okay, so there's a few things that need to take place prior to him coming here, so we would need to have an assessment completed for him, so that means I would have to speak with him personally to find out what substances he's using, and for sober living, that program we do not have at the moment, so if he's in active addiction, then we'll be able to get an assessment done for him, but he would need to speak with us personally to do that, and then the next step would be for us to verify his benefits, so he has to have active insurance, so if he does not have active insurance, he would need to go to the nearest hospital and sign up for PE, which is presumptive eligibility, that is like temporary insurance, and then after that, we can, once those boxes have been checked, then we can go ahead and schedule for admission, but we can't do that until I actually have to conduct a pre-assessment with the patient. Okay, active insurance with Medicaid qualified? Medicaid would qualify, absolutely. All right, I know while incarcerated he was approved for Medicaid, he just needs to activate his Medicaid card, I believe, and he hasn't been able to do that being that he's incarcerated. Okay. Well, I mean, we can screen to find out if it's active. If he's approved for Medicaid, it's not much that he needs to activate. It's not like a debit card that he has to just, you know, go turn it on. If he's approved, that means he's approved, so I would just need some information from him, date of birth, his full or correct spelling of his first and last name, and maybe his member ID number, and once we have that, we can screen for that to see, even if he doesn't have the card. If he has the information, I can still screen for that, but I need to do a pre-assessment with him prior to doing that, so that would be the biggest thing is to actually having a conversation with him while he's incarcerated. I know that's kind of hard to do, so that would only lead to one option. When he gets released, that means he would need to call us to conduct that pre-assessment over the phone and go through that process. Okay. All right. Could I possibly arrange that pre-assessment between you guys? Okay. Is that something that we could do prior to, like, while he's incarcerated? I mean, I know the phone calls are limited to 15 minutes. I assume it's going to take more time than that. Sometimes a pre-assessment can take up to 30 minutes, depending on if there's an issue with a screening for insurance or if we need to request for more information from that patient. So it could take up to 30 minutes. Okay. Up to 30. All right. So you said you don't have sober living. No, not at the moment. We do not have a sober living program here. Okay. Can I ask you a question? Sure. Do you know for sure if your brother is in active addiction? I know that prior to being arrested he was using crack cocaine. Okay. So that would be considered, like, a forced detox that he has to do. Do you know how long he's been incarcerated for? Since November 17th. Okay. And for us that would be considered, like, a forced detox. So he will be able to qualify here, just depending on if insurance is going to pay for that. But depending on how long he's been sober or whenever we drug screen him, what's in his urine, we'll be able to make that determination whether he could just go into our residential living program or go into our detox program. So that's totally up to, you know, like I said, the pre-assessment comes down to what information he gives me. And once we complete the process, he's going to have to take a urine screen and what we find in his urine. So we don't really care about what we care about what's in his urine, but we're not looking to, you know, disqualify someone because they have something in their urine. We need to know what it is. Okay. And if his urine is clean, is that the presenter problem? No, if his urine is clean, yeah. That means he would not have to stay into blackout, which is our detox period for seven days to have, you know, allow our patients to get rid of those substances. So if he has come here and he actually has no substances in his urine, we can go ahead and just place him into residential. And that's a 20 to 30-day program. Okay. All right. Yep. Okay. All right. Thank you very much for all this information. Yeah, I guess I just don't want him to get out and be put in a position to where he could relapse if we can avoid it. Yeah. I totally agree. Currently my living situation isn't able to let him come stay with me so that I can ensure that. So question for you. When he is released, where are they taking him? As far as I know, according to him, they'll take him wherever he wants pretty much. They will get him a Uber ride wherever he needs to go. But I had planned on picking him up, and he's very adamant about going in with you guys. Okay. So can I refer you to someone for him? That is a referral source for us that we use all the time. And until, like, he can just stay the night there, and then we can get him here as soon as possible. Instead of him being, you know, dropped off at, like, the town's homeless shelter where there's a lot of drug activity and, you know, a lot of triggers, I know a place called Rice Recovery that has open beds, and he can just stay there. It's a place where they don't tolerate, you know, drugs and alcohol and things like that. You come in and you get the help you need. He can stay there overnight until the next day when he wants to come in to try to admit with us. So I think that would be great for him. It all depends. Like, even if he gets out, like, as soon as he gets out, he can jump on the phone and call and do an assessment. We can do all of that stuff over the phone as soon as possible, and we can get him in the same day. So it also depends on what your availability is as well, because I know you said you're picking him up, but if you want him to come the same day, I just suggest that as soon as you pick him up, you give us a call. We do this assessment, run his insurance, get all of that squared away, and you can drop him off here with us. Okay. Yeah, that'd be great. Okay. Yeah, that'll be easier for you. Mm-hmm. So I had some questions about, as far as him, the accommodations you guys provide. Like, would he just bring his clothes? Does he need money for food? Oh, no, no, no, no. No, he doesn't need any money for food or anything. We provide meals, three meals a day, and also on top of that we provide snacks in between meals. But if he wants to have cash on him, just because we do store runs for our patients as well, so if he wants a specific snack or something like that, he can have a few dollars for that. But it's not like he has to have money. It's not like he has to pay for his meals because that's not true. Okay. We provide meals for him. We provide toiletry items for him. If he wants to bring in any, like, soaps or lotions and things, just make sure the first three ingredients does not contain alcohol. He'll be able to bring those in unopened. Okay. And he just needs enough clothes for at least 30 days. We have – he can launder his own clothes on the unit. He can wash his own stuff. So we have that available as well. And he won't need anything but just, like, an ID. And if he has his insurance information, bring that. If he has a Social Security card, bring that. And if he doesn't have any of that, that's still fine. We'll still accept him. Okay, because I know that his car had gotten impounded and he lost a lot of stuff because of that. And I don't know that he – I don't even know that he has his wallet or ID or anything like that. But I would imagine he has to have some kind of identification, being that he got Medicaid. Yeah, absolutely. Yeah, he has – as long as he has, like, if he knows his social and he knows his birthday and I just have the correct spelling of his name, that would be fine to pull insurance, depending on – like, the only way to give me a problem is if he has a common name and a common birthday. That's going to, like, throw me for a loop. But it's not something that we can't ever find. You know, we'll be able to find the insurance. It's not a problem. Because if he got it in while he was incarcerated, absolutely he has insurance. He just hasn't used it yet. That's all. It would be helpful for him to kind of, like, get that information now. Whenever you talk to him prior to him being released, get all of that information from him. They can give him – whoever he works with him, social worker or whoever that's working there on his case can provide him with that medical insurance information. So that way when he leaves, he'll have what he needs. All right, social worker. Insurance. All right. He currently doesn't have a vehicle, and there's a – he does have another pending court case in Lake County, Indiana, that he might need to make an appearance for. Does that pose an issue with you guys? We transport. So if the patient needs to – as long as it's not within the first seven days of him being here, we can work with the patient to kind of get that squared away. Usually what I know for sure is that our case managers can connect with the courts to kind of see, like, since this patient is in treatment, can we, like, reschedule this until he gets out? I've seen it done before. So as long as he's not scheduled that court day within the first seven days of him admitting, then we can work with that. We can make sure that if it's something that he cannot miss, he'll be transported by one of our staff members because we don't allow them to travel alone. And if not – if that's not available, then the case manager will work to get that rescheduled. Okay. All right, let me just make sure I've checked off all my – okay, Yes, active Medicaid member ID number, forced detox program. He doesn't need money for food. He doesn't need cash. Just needs to bring clothes. So no need for him to bring anything like toiletries or anything like that, you said? Not unless he has a specific kind that he likes to use, he can bring those. As long as they're unopened and the first three ingredients does not contain alcohol. Oh, do you allow nicotine use, smoking? Mm-hmm. Okay, nicotine. Yes, we have a smoking – we allow our patients to smoke and vape. We have a designated area where they can do that. All right, well, you know, you've been so helpful. Thank you very much. You're very welcome. When I pick them up, I think that's the route we're going to go, is we're going to try to get the pre-assessment done over the phone while we're driving to you guys. Okay, that's even better. Worst case, maybe we might have to look into this RISE recovery for an overnight stay, but – and that is also in Fort Wayne? Yes, it is. Do you want me to give you the address? Yeah, that'd be great just to have it on hand. Okay, one second. Let me pull it up. I want to give you the correct address. Oh, okay. So whenever you're ready, I have the address for you. I'm ready. 226. 226. West Wallace Street, W-A-L-L-A-C-E. So that's Wallace Street, Fort Wayne, Indiana, 46802. I can give you the phone number, too, just in case. That'd be great. Okay, 260. 260. 999. 999. 3-0-0-5. 3-0-0-5. Yep. Okay. Sorry, I'm taking notes just to make sure I'm not forgetting anything. No, you're fine. That's okay. So if he felt like he needed to stay beyond the 28 or 30 days, would that be an option for him? So with that, they would – so prior to him getting discharged, once he gets ready to complete the program, if he does well in the residential and insurance is okay with extending his stay based on his progress here, then they would allow him to go into our PHP program. So we have a PHP program with boarding here, meaning they can stay up to six to nine extra weeks, depending on how insurance wants to pay for that. So it depends on how well he does in his groups and if he has the willingness to take his sobriety serious, then they'll be able to push for him to go into the PHP program, which will allow him to stay roughly between six to nine weeks, sometimes longer, depending on everybody's need is different. So that would be a question that once he gets here, case management can really delve into for you. I'm just going to give you the basics. So, yeah, we do have an extended program here, but not everybody qualifies. So that would be something that if I were him looking into once he gets here, speak to a case manager. Okay. That's not good behavior. Well, progress. Okay. I think I've asked to take one second. Oh, if he wanted to seek employment while with you guys, is that a possibility? Not during the residential, not during the 28 to 30 days. He would not be able to work. But after he completes that and he is looking to go into and continue on with his sobriety, and he is actually approved to go into our PHP program, then he will absolutely be able to work as long it does not interfere with the group's schedule hours, which is 8 a.m. to 1 p.m. Tuesdays through Fridays. So he can work a second and a third shift. He just cannot miss those group days. That's the requirement for the program. Okay. 28 to 30 days. And he says 8 a.m. to 1 p.m. Yep, 1 p.m. Tuesdays through Fridays. Yep. Okay. What was your name again? Sherinda. Sherinda, my name is Matthew. You've been so helpful to me, and I really do appreciate all your time. Yeah, no problem. I'm going to relay all this to him, and hopefully we'll be seeing you soon. And if you don't mind, if I've got any more questions, I'll call you back. Yeah, please do. Call me back. It's not a problem. I'm here every day between 9 a.m. and 6 p.m., Monday through Friday. We live actually in Lansing. We're about an hour or so away. Okay. So we here, Elevate, we don't offer inpatient. What we have here is called PHP or day programming, and basically what that is is you're in group seven days a week from 8 in the morning until 4.30 in the afternoon. We do offer what we call dorm style living during that time, so people who are from out of town or have lack of transportation or other treatment barriers can stay in those dorms while they attend our program. But if he's looking for, like, an inpatient type stay, the places here would be, like, Pathways, Horizons, and what you would need to do is call them and schedule an assessment, and then they would be able to tell you when he can get into treatment. Now, if he experiences detox or withdrawal symptoms, excuse me, I would recommend that he go through the ER and go through detox at Mercy One in Waterloo, and during that time a substance use counselor will visit with him while he's in detox and do an assessment and could make a referral to inpatient. Okay. I will try and figure that out. So, yeah, but if he, you know, and then the other thing, too, is if he goes through inpatient and still needs support, he's still allowed to, like, do that PHP program with us, and we work with Horizons and Pathways with referrals to that as well. Does he vomit? Hallucinations? Sweats? Okay. So he definitely would probably need the medical detox before he would be able to go into any inpatient just to protect him from having any sort of, like, seizures or any other health issues that can occur when withdrawing.
